evaluation.manager_summary: Felicia handled a post-overdose crisis call with exceptional empathy, urgency recognition, and operational execution. She immediately validated the caller's survival, captured essential information efficiently, and escalated to medical intake within minutes with clear safety instructions. Compliance note: she provided medical necessity rationale ('body has been through significant stress') that should have been deferred to clinical staff, though the overall crisis response was appropriate and likely life-preserving.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent immediately acknowledged the life-threatening situation with warmth and urgency ('I am so glad you called and I am so glad your roommate was there'). Used caller's name multiple times, matched the emotional register perfectly, and checked on physical state before proceeding—exemplary crisis empathy.

Agent captured carrier name, member ID, and group number efficiently. The collection was appropriately brief given the crisis context. Minor note: didn't explicitly verify plan type, though this is excusable given the urgent medical situation requiring immediate escalation.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: In same-day crisis admissions, consider capturing plan type even in abbreviated format for downstream verification.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent captured substance type (fentanyl) and duration of use (eight months). Given the overdose context and immediate escalation priority, the screening was appropriately focused. Did not ask about prior treatment or withdrawal symptoms, but the crisis situation justified moving directly to medical escalation.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Even in crisis escalations, consider one quick question about prior treatment history if time allows—helps medical team prepare.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent immediately recognized the overdose as a same-day medical emergency and escalated appropriately. Checked ER status, arranged medical evaluation 'today—not tomorrow—today', coordinated immediate escalation to medical intake, and ensured caller safety ('Do not drive. Do not be alone'). Textbook crisis response.
Caller is the patient herself, not a family member.

Caller expressed fear about going to the ER. Agent acknowledged it ('I understand') before pivoting to honest medical guidance and immediate escalation path. The response was empathetic but appropriately directive given the crisis context—no dismissiveness, no high pressure, just clear care.

Agent provided crystal-clear next steps: medical intake coordinator will call back in under ten minutes, transport will be coordinated, caller must stay with roommate and not drive. Timing, ownership, and action all explicitly stated.

Agent crossed into clinical scope multiple times: stated 'after an opioid overdose, medical evaluation is important—not just for the overdose itself, but because your body has been through significant stress' (explaining medical necessity without deferral), and characterized the situation as 'genuine medical emergency' (clinical assessment). These are clinical determinations that should be deferred to medical staff.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: In overdose situations, validate the seriousness and escalate immediately, but defer medical necessity explanations to clinical staff ('our medical team will assess what's needed').

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Kristin, I am so glad you called and I am so glad your roommate was there. That was a life-saving moment and you survived it.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: How are you feeling physically right now?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Good. You're okay.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Okay. Do you have insurance with you?

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Yes. CoreFirst Insurance. My card is here. Member ID BP82630580, group GRP-815784.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I have that. Kristin, I'm escalating you to our medical intake coordinator right now

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Can I ask: are you using heroin, fentanyl, or prescription opioids?

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Fentanyl. I've been using for about eight months.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Eight months of fentanyl use and an overdose last night — this is a genuine medical emergency

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: First — have you been seen by a doctor since last night? Did you go to the ER?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I want to get you both medically evaluated and into our detox program today. Not tomorrow — today.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Do not drive. Do not be alone. Can you do that?

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: No. My roommate wanted me to but I refused. I was scared.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I understand. I'm going to be honest with you: after an opioid overdose, medical evaluation is important

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I want to get you both medically evaluated and into our detox program today. Not tomorrow — today.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I'm escalating you to our medical intake coordinator right now — they handle same-day admissions for exactly this kind of situation.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I need you to stay on the line. Does your roommate know you're calling us?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Tell them to stay with you until our coordinator calls back — which will be in under ten minutes.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: after an opioid overdose, medical evaluation is important — not just for the overdose itself, but because your body has been through significant stress.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Eight months of fentanyl use and an overdose last night — this is a genuine medical emergency, Kristin

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I'd like to arrange for you to be seen at our medical facility this morning.
